title_slide: NumPy: The Art of Thinking in Arrays

slides.heading: NumPy: The Art of Thinking in Arrays
bullet_points: The mental shift that changes everything
slides.speaker_note: Frame this as a conceptual upgrade, not just a new library to memorize.

slides.heading: The Performance Problem
slides.speaker_note: Lead with the concrete benchmark so the why is clear before showing any code.

slides.heading: What NumPy Arrays Are
bullet_points: All elements must be the same type
Stored in contiguous memory — no pointer-chasing
Operations run in compiled C and Fortran
slides.speaker_note: Explain the tradeoff explicitly: less flexibility, far more performance.

slides.heading: Creating Arrays: 3 Ways
bullet_points: np.linspace(0, 1, 6) — fixed number of evenly spaced points
slides.speaker_note: Highlight dtype in the output — it's the first visual difference from Python lists.

slides.heading: Vectorized Operations
bullet_points: One line, compiled execution, entire array at once
slides.speaker_note: This is the conceptual core — show the before/after loop comparison explicitly.

slides.heading: Boolean Indexing
bullet_points: Same syntax Pandas uses: df[df['col'] > value]
slides.speaker_note: Emphasize that learning this here means Pandas filtering won't require a new mental model.

slides.heading: Aggregations and Outlier Detection
slides.speaker_note: Walk through the IQR outlier code — q25, q75, upper/lower fence, boolean filter.

slides.heading: 2D Arrays and the Axis Problem
bullet_points: axis=0 collapses down; axis=1 collapses across
slides.speaker_note: Use the mnemonic: 0 down, 1 across — it resolves the confusion faster than explanation.
